---
title: "Seemingly irrelevant decisions"
format: html
---

## Seemingly Irrelevant Decisions

By paying more attention to the decision making process, you will have a greater chance to interrupt the chain of decisions that could lead to a relapse. This is important because it is much easier to stop the process early, before you wind up in a high-risk situation, than later when you are in a situation that is harder to handle and may expose you to a number of triggers.

Seemingly Irrelevant Decisions are choices that may appear harmless or unrelated to substance use on the surface, but that gradually move you closer to situations where relapse becomes more likely. These decisions often happen in small steps and can feel easy to justify in the moment. For example, deciding to take a different route home that passes a familiar bar, agreeing to attend an event where alcohol will be present, or reconnecting with someone associated with past use may not seem risky at first, but they can increase exposure to cravings, triggers, or temptation. Recognizing these choices early can help you make safer decisions before risk builds.

Also, by paying attention to your decision making process, you will be able to recognize certain kinds of thoughts that can lead to making risky decisions. Thoughts like “I have to” go to a party, “should” see a certain drinking buddy, or “have to” drive by a particular place, often occur at the beginning of a Seemingly Irrelevant Decision and should be treated as a warning or red flag. Other red-flag thoughts often start with “It doesn’t matter if I . . . ,” “I can handle . . . ,” and so on.

![](https://github.com/jjcurtin/aud_support/blob/main/modules/images/fork.jpg?raw=true){fig-align="center"}
